text: Not all movement is cognitively equivalent. Research by Charles Hillman at the University of Illinois distinguishes between aerobic and non-aerobic physical activity in their neural effects. A session of walking on a treadmill consistently produces measurable improvements in executive function — the cluster of abilities governing attention, impulse control, and working memory — while equivalent time spent in stretching or resistance training at low intensity does not reliably produce the same acute boost. The difference appears to hinge on cardiovascular demand: sustained elevation of heart rate stimulates the molecular cascade that reshapes prefrontal activity. Effort, not merely motion, is the active ingredient.
question: According to the passage, what distinguishes aerobic activity from low-intensity resistance training in its cognitive effects?

options.text: Aerobic activity targets hippocampal volume while resistance training targets the prefrontal cortex
Sustained cardiovascular demand from aerobic activity stimulates the molecular cascade affecting executive function
Resistance training produces stronger BDNF effects than aerobic exercise over time
Both forms of exercise produce identical improvements in working memory
